judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit all five expectations with equivalent core citations; Report 2 is somewhat more thorough (independent verification of the 99.92% figure, fuller timeline, and an added hedging critique) but coverage of the required expectations is comparable.

report_1.evidence: failures began at 14:03, the first page didn't fire until 14:50 (47 minutes later), and on-call didn't acknowledge until 15:05 (62 minutes after failures began). This is not detection "within minutes" nor an "immediate" response

report_2.evidence: `larkpay-comms/incidents/OPS-412-timeline.md` shows failures began at 14:03 but the first page didn't fire until 14:50 (47 minutes later), and on-call didn't acknowledge until 15:05 — full recovery wasn't until 15:41, 98 minutes after failures began.

report_1.evidence: `larkpay-comms/incidents/OPS-412-timeline.md` states: "During the window 1,284 transactions failed and required manual reprocessing; 37 were unrecoverable and refunded."

report_2.evidence: `larkpay-comms/incidents/OPS-412-timeline.md:9-10` states 1,284 transactions failed and required manual reprocessing, and 37 were unrecoverable and refunded.

report_1.evidence: `larkpay-comms/docs/sla-commitments.md` sets the contractual monthly uptime SLA at 99.9%, with any month below that being a breach that "owes service credits per the customer agreement." `larkpay-comms/metrics/uptime-2026.csv` shows April 2026 uptime at 99.77% — below the 99.9% threshold, i.e., an actual contractual breach.

report_2.evidence: `larkpay-comms/docs/sla-commitments.md` sets a contractual monthly uptime SLA of 99.9%, breach of which owes service credits scaling with the depth of breach. `larkpay-comms/metrics/uptime-2026.csv` shows April 2026 (`2026-04,99.77`) at 99.77%, below the 99.9% threshold — a contractual breach.

report_1.evidence: `larkpay-comms/docs/customer-comms-style-guide.md` rule 1 says "Never assign blame to an individual" and rule 2 says "Never expose internal system or role names." The draft's line "The outage was caused by the on-call engineer deploying an untested config change... interacted badly with the checkout autoscaler" violates both

report_2.evidence: `larkpay-comms/docs/customer-comms-style-guide.md` rule 1 says "Never assign blame to an individual" and rule 2 says "Never expose internal system or role names." The draft states the outage "was caused by the on-call engineer deploying an untested config change" and that it "interacted badly with the checkout autoscaler"

report_1.evidence: `larkpay-comms/docs/customer-comms-style-guide.md` rule 3 requires leading with customer impact and remediation. The current draft opens with a self-congratulatory uptime claim ("LarkPay has maintained 99.99% uptime...") before any mention of impact — this ordering needs to be fixed.

report_2.evidence: style guide rule 3 ("Lead with customer impact and remediation") is violated: the draft leads with a self-congratulatory uptime claim rather than actual customer impact, and never states any real impact figures.
